reasoning: For the mother row (dema, an dhama, dhamow, [a.], mother), the pattern for definite plural is 'an' + plural form without mutation, as seen in examples like an benenes and an karesow. Thus, definite plural is an dhamow.

For the mare row, the definite singular is given as an gasek, indicating soft mutation k > g. Therefore, the singular is kasek, following Celtic language patterns where the definite article causes initial mutation.

For the mare row, the definite plural is an kasegi (given in text), so the plural form is kasegi without mutation after 'an' in plural, similar to mowysi for girls. Plural formation adds -i, akin to mowes > mowysi.
For the mare row, the definite plural follows the pattern 'an' + plural, as in all examples (e.g., an kwilkynow). Thus, it is an kasegi, matching the given text.
For the king row (myghtern, [e.], myghternedh, an vghternedh, king), definite singular mutates m > v after 'an', as in an venyn and an vowes. Thus, an vghtern.
For the queen row, similar to king, singular myghternes mutates m > v after 'an' for definite singular, following the pattern for feminine nouns like mother an dhama.
For the queen row, definite plural is 'an' + plural myghternesow without mutation, as plurals do not mutate after 'an' (e.g., an mowysi starts with m).
For the billy-goat row (tas, [h.], tasow, [i.], billy-goat), definite singular mutates t > d after 'an', as in adult man an devesik and patterns for t-initial words.
For the billy-goat row, definite plural is 'an' + plural tasow without mutation, consistent with plural examples like an tasow (no change from tasow).
For the sow row (bogh, [j.], boghes, [k.], sow), definite singular mutates b > v after 'an', as in an venyn for woman and an vanow for similar words.
For the sow row, definite plural 'an' + plural boghes without mutation, following plural pattern (e.g., an badhes for boar).
For the banow row (banow, [l.], banowes, [m.], sow or pig), definite singular mutates b > v, consistent with b-initial feminine nouns like an venyn.
For the banow row, definite plural 'an' + plural banowes without mutation, as in other plurals like an ponsyow.
For the boar row (badh, an badh, [n.], an badhes, boar), the plural is badhes, following +es formation as in davas > deves and benyn > benenes.
For the adult man row (tevesik, [o.], [p.], an devesigyon, adult man), definite singular mutates t > d after 'an', as in an das for tas.
For 'the finger', bys means finger from part 2 (bys byhan pinky finger). Definite article 'an' causes b > v mutation for b-initial words like benyn > an venyn, so an vys.

For 'on a beautiful cake', cake is tesen (from part 1 s. tesen, an desen). After 'war', t > d mutation (war desennow for cakes in part 2). Beautiful is teg (men teg beautiful stone in part 2), no further mutation needed.
For 'on a door', door is daras (daras melyn yellow door in part 2). After 'war', d > dh mutation, as in war dhavas on a sheep (davas > dhavas).

an bal bervdh: bal means mine (an bal byhan the small mine in part 2). bervdh is berfdh 'perfect' (war bluwen berfdh on a perfect pen in part 2), with possible spelling variation or mutation p > b in adjective.
das appears in war dhas deg 'on a beautiful stack' in part 2, so das means 'stack' (mutated form after war, t > d from underlying tas?).
war das is the phrase from part 2 war dhas deg 'on a beautiful stack', so without adjective, 'on a stack'.

The English word 'penguin' derives from the Welsh phrase 'pen gwyn' meaning 'white head', referring originally to the great auk with white spots on its head. Welsh is closely related to Cornish, both Brythonic Celtic languages.
